skill.name: codebase-analysis
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through structured analysis of existing codebases to recover domain models, business rules, integrations, workflows, and user capabilities.
skill.summary: Analyze existing codebases to document business logic, domain models, integrations, and workflows.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network patterns, but review found they are documentation examples and Markdown syntax rather than executable skill logic. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior was found in SKILL.md or patterns.md. The remaining risk is low because the skill teaches codebase exploration techniques and includes illustrative command examples.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown examples flagged as command execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are caused by Markdown code fences, inline code terms, architecture diagrams, and sample grep searches. These lines are instructional documentation, not executable scripts, and the skill only declares Read, Grep, Glob, and LSP tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visibly inside Markdown examples or inline code markers. No script file, shell invocation wrapper, or user-controlled command execution path exists in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False positive: Weak cryptography keyword matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with ordinary words and documentation examples, not calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak algorithms. No cryptographic operation is implemented by this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain prose, table headings, or architecture guidance. I did not find evidence of cryptographic library usage or weak algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: False positive: Illustrative network endpoint
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is an example payment gateway endpoint inside an output artifact template. It does not instruct the assistant to contact the URL or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears within a Markdown documentation example for integration analysis. There is no network-capable tool declaration or executable request logic tied to this endpoint.

low_findings.title: Low risk: Codebase exploration guidance can expose local project details
low_findings.description: The skill asks assistants to inspect project structure, entities, permissions, integrations, and workflows. This is expected behavior for codebase analysis, but users should apply it only to repositories they are allowed to analyze.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it intentionally guides broad local code inspection. The risk depends on repository authorization and handling of sensitive project information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed pattern: Backticks and grep examples in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis interpreted Markdown backticks and grep examples as shell execution. Review found no executable shell script or command wrapper in the skill files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context is Markdown documentation. The skill does not include code that runs shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed pattern: Example external URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The payment API URL is sample documentation for identifying integrations. It is not used for an outbound request or data transfer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is embedded in a Markdown output example. No network call implementation exists in SKILL.md or patterns.md.

content.value_statement: Legacy systems often hide important requirements inside code, tests, and configuration. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured process for extracting business rules and system behavior.
actual_capabilities: Maps project structure, entry points, layers, and major components.
Identifies domain entities, relationships, validation rules, and workflows.
Documents API endpoints, integrations, data flows, and authentication patterns.
Recognizes common architecture patterns such as layered, clean, CQRS, and event-driven designs.
Creates analysis artifacts for domain models, business rules, integrations, and user capabilities.
Provides search heuristics for C#, Java, TypeScript, REST, GraphQL, and validation patterns.
limitations: It provides analysis guidance, not automated execution or complete static analysis tooling.
Results depend on the assistant having access to relevant files and project context.
It may miss runtime behavior that is only visible in production data or external systems.
It does not replace expert review for security, compliance, or legal requirements.

use_cases.title: Recover Requirements From Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Identify entities, workflows, and rules when documentation is missing or outdated.

use_cases.title: Prepare Brownfield Modernization
use_cases.description: Map architecture, integrations, and technical constraints before refactoring or migration work.

use_cases.title: Document Product Capabilities
use_cases.description: Translate existing API, UI, and permission logic into clear capability documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Map the Project Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of an unfamiliar repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository structure. Identify the main layers, entry points, configuration files, and likely business domains.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Domain Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Find core business objects and relationships.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the model, entity, schema, and migration files. Document the core domain entities, key attributes, relationships, and constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Document Business Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Find validation logic, service methods, state transitions, and calculations. Summarize each business rule with its implementation location and conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Capability Map
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis across APIs, UI, permissions, and integrations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace user-facing capabilities from UI or API entry points through services, permissions, data stores, and external integrations. Produce a capability map with evidence.

output_examples.input: Analyze the order management module for business rules.
output: Order total must equal the sum of active line items.
Submitted orders can move to approved or cancelled states only.
Approval requires a manager role and a valid payment method.
Evidence should include service methods, validators, and endpoint handlers.

output_examples.input: Create an integration summary for the payment flow.
output: Payment is called after order submission and before fulfillment.
The integration sends order amount and payment token data.
Failures leave the order pending payment and require retry handling.
Relevant evidence includes the payment adapter, queue handler, and API contract.

output_examples.input: Summarize user capabilities from controllers and permissions.
output: Customers can create, view, and cancel draft orders.
Managers can approve submitted orders and review exceptions.
Administrators can configure product limits and payment settings.

best_practices: Start with entry points, then trace behavior through services, domain logic, and persistence.
Record evidence locations for every inferred rule, workflow, and integration.
Separate confirmed behavior from assumptions that require product or engineering review.
anti_patterns: Do not treat folder names alone as proof of business behavior.
Do not document rules without linking them to implementation evidence.
Do not analyze repositories that you are not authorized to inspect.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It guides analysis of code structure, domain models, business rules, workflows, integrations, and user capabilities.

faq.question: Does it run automated scanners?
faq.answer: No. It provides structured analysis methods and search heuristics for an AI assistant.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it replace product discovery?
faq.answer: No. It helps recover implemented behavior, but stakeholders should confirm intent and missing requirements.

faq.question: Is it suitable for legacy systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for brownfield analysis where documentation may be incomplete.

faq.question: What should users provide?
faq.answer: Users should provide repository access, target modules, and the business questions they need answered.
